title: REA lets AI add flair to listings
full_text:
Digital property company REA Group is betting it will win from the AI revolution as it draws on technologies to boost services for property buyers and agencies.
REA chief executive Cameron McIntyre said there was now a greater focus on immersive consumer experiences, and increasing the value that was delivered to customers.
The company’s tools mean that it is able to boost its level of engagement with prospective buyers. Tools such as conversational search are allowing a deeper connection with REA customers, he said.
REA reported a healthy March quarter – despite rising interest rates and the Middle East conflict – with the result underpinned by double-digit revenue growth in its Australian businesses, and strong double-digit yield growth in its core residential business.
“Strong underlying fundamentals supported the health of the property market and supply kept pace with buyer demand,” Mr McIntyre said.
“While global events and interest rate increases impacted broader economic sentiment, listing activity in the two largest property markets, Sydney and Melbourne, remained strong.” Property portal realestate.com.au hit a new quarterly record of 12.9 million average monthly visitors. New data this quarter showed REA attracts and engages the buyer for nine in 10 properties sold on its platform.
Mr McIntyre said that with more normalised levels of buyer demand, agents and property sellers will be seeking to differentiate their listings, with REA well positioned in the “more balanced” market.
“Increasingly, our product features are powered by AI, accelerating our speed to market and enhancing the experiences we deliver,” he said.
April listing volumes were up 19 per cent year-on-year, with Melbourne increasing 20 per cent and Sydney up 25 per cent, reflecting the easier comparables as flagged previously.
REA had a healthy March quarter with its core operations performing well even as market conditions softened, hitting property values in Sydney and Melbourne.
Excluding acquisitions, REA’s core revenue increased by 11 per cent, or $398m, while core operating expenses rose 5 per cent to $178m on the same basis. Core earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortisation, excluding associates, was up 16 per cent to $220m.
Residential revenue was up 12 per cent driven by strong buy yield growth of 14 per cent. National buy listing volumes returned to growth, up 1 per cent, with Sydney up 4 per cent and Melbourne up 7 per cent.
REA left its expectations of a 1 per cent to 3 per cent decline in national residential buy listing volumes unchanged. It anticipates residential buy yield growth of about 13 per cent and positive operating jaws this financial year for both Australia and across the company.
REA lowered cost guidance for fiscal 2026, with group operating costs now expected to increase by low to mid single digits and Australian costs by mid to high single-digits. REA unveiled a $200m share buyback in February and has since bought back $75.5m of stock. REA shares added 1.4 per cent to $176.89.
summary: REA Group is leveraging AI to enhance property listings and customer engagement, reporting strong growth despite challenging market conditions.

heading: Data Science and AI
key_points: REA's tools, powered by AI, are improving engagement with property buyers and agencies.
The company reported a 12% increase in residential revenue and a 19% year-on-year rise in listing volumes.
REA anticipates continued growth in residential buy yield and has lowered its cost guidance for fiscal 2026.
relevance: This is relevant to actuaries and financial services professionals as it highlights the impact of technology on market dynamics and revenue growth in the property sector.
